title: First-Principles Effective Mass in the Three-Dimensional Uniform Electron Gas
authors: Pengcheng Hou, Daniel Cerkoney, Zhiyi Li, Tao Wang, Xiansheng Cai, Lei Wang, Gabriel Kotliar, Youjin Deng, Kun Chen
abstract: The quasiparticle effective mass $m^*$ of the three-dimensional uniform electron gas (UEG) is a fundamental Fermi-liquid parameter whose value and density dependence have remained controversial for decades. Using renormalized perturbation theory with explicit counterterms, we determine $m^*$ in the metallic regime ($r_s \le 6$) from first principles by two complementary routes -- the self-energy and the forward-scattering four-point vertex via the $p$-wave spin-symmetric Landau parameter $F_1^s$ -- that agree within uncertainties at each density through sixth renormalized order. The resulting $m^*/m$ remains close to unity throughout the metallic regime, with a shallow non-monotonic density dependence -- a minimum near $r_s\approx 1$ followed by a gentle upturn -- reflecting the interplay of exchange and dynamical screening in the self-energy, and disfavoring strong monotonic suppression. This finding supports a physical picture for the metallic UEG in which dominant charge correlations are concentrated in nearly forward scattering and generate only a weak $F_1^s$ component.
summary: This paper provides a high-precision, first-principles calculation of the effective mass in a 3D electron gas using sixth-order renormalized perturbation theory. By using two consistent computational routes, the authors show that the effective mass stays near unity and exhibits a non-monotonic density dependence. This result resolves previous discrepancies between different many-body theoretical approaches.
main_problem: Resolving the long-standing controversy regarding the value and density dependence of the quasiparticle effective mass (m*) in the three-dimensional uniform electron gas (3D UEG).
main_result: The effective mass ratio m*/m remains close to unity throughout the metallic regime (rs <= 6) with a shallow, non-monotonic density dependence, disfavoring strong monotonic suppression.
method: Renormalized perturbation theory using sixth-order diagrams, employing Taylor-mode automatic differentiation and Monte Carlo integration via a dual-route approach (self-energy and four-point vertex).
model_or_system: A three-dimensional uniform electron gas (3D UEG) treated as a Landau Fermi liquid with long-ranged Coulomb interactions, specifically in the metallic regime (rs <= 6).
key_observables: Quasiparticle effective mass ratio (m*/m), quasiparticle residue (Z), dispersion renormalization (D), and the spin-symmetric Landau parameter (F1s).
important_parameters: Wigner-Seitz density parameter (rs), renormalized order (xi), and auxiliary screening parameter (lambda_R).
main_assumptions: The expansion is carried out to the sixth renormalized order; results are extrapolated from finite temperature (T/TF = 1/40) to zero temperature.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the new m*/m results against various literature estimates and diagrammatic approximations, showing near-unity behavior. Table I provides a detailed numerical comparison of the two computational routes and other methods.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the controversy of effective mass, describes the renormalized perturbation theory framework and the dual-route computational approach, presents the results for the metallic regime, and concludes with a discussion on the physical origin of the observed mass behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quasiparticle effective mass ratio m∗/m of the uni- form electron gas versus rs. Black circles show the final values quoted in this work; the route-resolved self-energy and ver- tex determinations are listed separately in Tab. I and agree within uncertainties at each rs. Other symbols show represen- tative literature estimates: variational diagrammatic Monte Carlo (VDMC) [22], diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) [20],and variational Monte Carlo (VMC) using different trial wave- functions [21]. Dashed curves illustrate standard diagram- matic approximations: G0W0 and two vertex-improved vari- ants (G0W+ and G0W±) [8]. FIG. 2. Schematic structure of the renormalized diagram- matic expansion, organized by powers of the bookkeeping pa- rameter ξ. (a) Self-energy Σ: the first-order Fock diagram and representative second-order contributions, including skeleton diagrams (two Yukawa lines) and counterterm insertions on the first-order graph. (b) Four-point vertex Γ4: the zeroth- order (tree-level) Yukawa exchange, representative first-order skeleton diagrams (particle-hole bubble and particle-particle ladder), and counterterm insertions.
